name: Kristang (Papia Kristang, Malacca Creole Portuguese)

allophone_rules.notes: DISABLES the inherited European-Portuguese Lisbon 'chiado' (coda /s/ → [ʃ]): Kristang has no /ʃ/ at all — coda /s/ stays [s], mas [mas] 'more' (Baxter 1988, p. 21: /s/ is the only fricative attested word-finally). Identity rewrite cancels the inherited rule by id.
DISABLES the inherited coda /z/ → [ʒ]: Kristang has no /ʒ/ (Baxter 1988, Table 2.1, p. 20). Identity rewrite.
DISABLES the inherited EP dark coda [ɫ]: Baxter transcribes plain [l] in codas — mal [mal] 'bad', mel [mɛl] 'honey' (Baxter 1988, pp. 23, 25). Identity rewrite.
Nasal+consonant clusters are homorganic: the nasal assimilates in place to the following consonant — kanggrezu [kaŋgrezu] 'crab' (Baxter 1988, A Grammar of Kristang, p. 22).
Homorganic nasal assimilation before labials — kambrang [kambraŋ] 'prawn' (Baxter 1988, p. 22).

ancestors.notes: Portuguese superstrate: lexifier from the Portuguese occupation of Malacca (1511-1641). Kristang preserves the OLDER Portuguese affricates /tʃ dʒ/ where modern Portuguese has fricatives /ʃ ʒ/ (Baxter 1988, ch. 2).

Malay substrate/adstrate: the consonant and vowel systems closely parallel Malay (Baxter 1988, pp. 20, 24, citing Onn 1980); Malay-based practical orthography (ny, ng, ch, j); syllabic nasals; homorganic NC clusters; [a] realizing both /a/ and /ə/ as in Malay.

notes: Kristang / Papia Kristang (Malacca Creole Portuguese), spoken by the Portuguese-Eurasian community of Malacca (Malaysia) and Singapore; severely endangered (<1,000 fluent speakers). This spec targets Baxter's Malay-based practical orthography (Baxter 1988; Baxter & de Silva 2004 dictionary): 5 vowel letters + p b t d k g m n ny ng r f v s z ch j l. KEY FEATURES: (1) 18 CONSONANTS, 8 ORAL VOWELS (Baxter 1988, p. 219: 'seven vowel phonemes' core /i e ɛ a ɔ o u/ + central /ə/); NO NASAL VOWELS — Portuguese nasal vowels denasalized to oral vowel + nasal coda (mão→mang, bom→bong). (2) NO /ʃ ʒ ʎ h/: the medieval Portuguese affricates /tʃ/ ⟨ch⟩ and /dʒ/ ⟨j⟩ are retained instead of the modern fricatives; /ʎ/→/l/ or /lj/ (filha→fila). (3) VOWEL LETTERS UNDER-DIFFERENTIATE: ⟨e⟩ covers /e ɛ ə/ and ⟨o⟩ covers /o ɔ/ — genuinely ambiguous; the /e/-/ɛ/ and /o/-/ɔ/ contrasts are low-functional-load and neutralize initially and finally (Baxter 1988, pp. 24-27). (4) SINGLE RHOTIC /r/: tap and trill in free variation (trill under emphasis), not positional (p. 23); coda /r/ unstable, often lost or metathesized (pertu ~ petru ~ petu). (5) /v/ MARGINAL (novi 'nine' vs nomi 'name', p. 22); historical Pt /v/→/b/ (novo→nubu). (6) HOMORGANIC NC CLUSTERS (kanggrezu, kambrang, kanji) and syllabic nasals (ngua 'one', nsentu 'hundred') — Malay-type traits (pp. 22, 49, 219). (7) FINAL CONSONANTS limited to /s r l m n ŋ/; final /ŋ/ ⟨ng⟩ common from denasalization. (8) STRESS: penultimate by default, phonemic (kaza 'house' vs kazá 'marry', p. 29); the acute accent is written only when stress is NOT penultimate (Baxter, APiCS survey ch. 42). Gemination unverified in accessible sources; treated as absent.

sources.author: Baxter, A. N.
sources.title: A Grammar of Kristang (Malacca Creole Portuguese)
sources.publisher: Pacific Linguistics Series B-95, Australian National University
sources.notes: Open access (CC BY-SA). Ch. 2 phonology: Table 2.1 consonants (p. 20), Table 2.2 vowels (pp. 23-24), vowel reduction (§2.3, pp. 29-30), contrastive stress (p. 29), inventory summary (p. 219). NOTE: the ANU scan is missing printed pp. 33-48 (§2.5 phonotactics, §2.6 stress, §2.7 orthography); those topics are cited here from Baxter's own APiCS survey instead.

sources.author: Baxter, A. N.
sources.title: Papiá Kristang (APiCS Online, survey chapter 42)
sources.publisher: In: Michaelis et al. (eds.), Atlas of Pidgin and Creole Language Structures, OUP
sources.notes: Practical orthography inventory (5 vowel letters + 18 consonant characters incl. ny, ng, ch, j); stress rule: 'Tonic stress is indicated on vowels and syllabic nasals by a written accent when the tonic does not fall on a penultimate syllable'; eight oral vowels.

sources.author: Baxter, A. N. & de Silva, P.
sources.title: A Dictionary of Kristang (Malacca Creole Portuguese) - English
sources.notes: Dictionary using the same Malay-based practical orthography this spec targets.

location.notes: Glottolog's representative point for Malacca-Batavia Portuguese Creole (Malacca).

stress.notes: Penultimate by default and unmarked (kaza [ˈkaza]); a written acute marks non-penultimate stress (kazá [kaˈza] 'marry', kumí 'eat') — Baxter, APiCS survey ch. 42; contrastive stress at Baxter 1988, p. 29. Diphthongs declared so pai [paj], pau [paw], noiba [ˈnɔjba], chua [ˈtʃua] count one nucleus each.

orthography_standard.name: Baxter's Malay-based practical orthography
orthography_standard.notes: 5 vowel letters and 18 consonant characters: a e i o u; p b t d k g m n ny ng r f v s z ch j l. Malay conventions: ny /ɲ/, ng /ŋ/, ch /tʃ/, j /dʒ/. No c, q, x, h, lh, nh. Acute accent only for non-penultimate stress. A rival Portuguese-based convention (⟨nh⟩ etc.) exists in community use; this spec follows Baxter's system, the one used by the reference dictionary.
